content:
Let \(N=7\cdot11\cdot13=1001\).  We want positive \(n<2018\) for which the three residues
\[r_7=n^2\pmod7,\quad r_{11}=n^2\pmod{11},\quad r_{13}=n^2\pmod{13}\]
are all equal (as ordinary integers).  In that case the common remainder \(r\) must lie in the overlap of \(\{0,1,\dots,6\}\), so \(r\in\{0,1,2,3,4,5,6\}\).  

For each prime \(p\in\{7,11,13\}\) and each \(r\) we let
\[
S_p(r)=\#\{x\pmod p:\;x^2\equiv r\pmod p\}.
\]
By Euler’s criterion for a prime \(p\), \(S_p(0)=1\), \(S_p(r)=2\) if \(r\) is a nonzero quadratic residue mod \(p\), and \(S_p(r)=0\) otherwise.  One checks:

– Mod 7 the nonzero squares are \(\{1,2,4\}\).  
– Mod 11 the nonzero squares are \(\{1,3,4,5,9\}\).  
– Mod 13 the nonzero squares are \(\{1,3,4,9,10,12\}\).

Hence for \(r=0,1,2,3,4,5,6\),
\[
\bigl(S_7(r),S_{11}(r),S_{13}(r)\bigr)=
\begin{cases}
(1,1,1)&r=0,\\
(2,2,2)&r=1,\\
(2,0,0)&r=2,\\
(0,2,2)&r=3,\\
(2,2,2)&r=4,\\
(0,2,0)&r=5,\\
(0,0,2)&r=6.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus only \(r=0,1,4\) give any solutions, and their counts modulo \(1001\) are
\[
r=0:\;1\quad(\text{namely }n\equiv0\pmod{1001}),\qquad
r=1:\;2\cdot2\cdot2=8,\qquad
r=4:\;2\cdot2\cdot2=8.
\]
In all there are \(1+8+8=17\) solutions modulo \(1001\).

Now \(1\le n<2018\) spans exactly two full residue‐classes modulo \(1001\) (that is \(1\le n\le1001\) and \(1002\le n\le2002\)), plus a tail \(2003\le n\le2017\) of 15 more integers.  Each full block of length 1001 contributes 17 solutions, so that gives \(2\times17=34\).  In the last block \(2003\le n\le2017\) one checks by hand (or by noticing it amounts to testing \(n\equiv1,\dots,15\pmod{1001}\)) that exactly two of those 15 satisfy the common‐remainder condition (\(n=2003\equiv1\) or \(2004\equiv2\)).  

Hence the total number of positive \(n<2018\) is
\[
34+2 \;=\;36.
\]
\boxed{36}
